metadata.file_name: 73. Yun et al. (2025). Discovering action insights from large-scale assessment log data using machine learning.pdf
metadata.title: Discovering action insights from large-scale assessment log data using machine learning

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses the 2012 PIAAC log data and focuses on two PIAAC problem sets (“Party Invitation” and “Club Membership”). The analytic sample is restricted to participants with complete action logs/sequences (incomplete logs or sequences with missing actions were removed) and then stratified by performance levels to compare high- vs low-performing groups. The manuscript does not report applying PIAAC sampling weights or any replicate-weight procedure (e.g., BRR/jackknife), and it does not name any weight variables. Because the core models are neural networks and random forests trained on vectorized action-sequence representations, the analysis appears to follow an ML workflow that does not incorporate complex survey weights; this omission may affect population representativeness under PIAAC’s complex sampling design.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: Plausible values (PVs) are not applicable because the dependent variables are performance levels derived from the PIAAC problem-set scores (e.g., score0 vs score3; correct vs incorrect), not PV-based cognitive achievement scales. Missing data handling is described as removing incomplete logs or sequences with missing actions, which corresponds to listwise deletion of affected sequences rather than PV-based multiple imputation.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used the 2012 PIAAC log data and restricted analyses to two problem sets: “Party Invitation” (35 distinct actions) and “Club Membership” (25 distinct actions). Preprocessed the data by removing incomplete logs or sequences with missing actions, then stratified the remaining sequences by performance levels to form high- vs low-performing groups for feature selection and verification. For “Party Invitation,” analyses focused on score0 vs score3 groups; for “Club Membership,” analyses focused on correct vs incorrect groups (with sorting actions excluded from feature verification due to low frequency).

main_findings.target_variable: High vs low performance (score3 vs score0)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Neural Network (Doc2Vec vectors): F1 0.850–0.962 and Accuracy 0.850–0.946 across cases; best case (Case 2: mail-moving actions only) Accuracy 94.6% and F1 0.962. Random Forest: F1 0.893–0.951 and Accuracy 0.852–0.930; best case (Case 2) Accuracy 92.3% and F1 0.949. Unsupervised verification: silhouette score 0.362 (Case 1 all actions), 0.491 (Case 2 mail-moving only), 0.216 (Case 3 without mail-moving).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PIAAC 2012 “Party Invitation” log the study leveraged action-sequence representations (Word2Vec/Doc2Vec) to predict high vs low performance (score3 vs score0), finding that focusing on mail-moving actions (Case 2) produced the strongest separation (silhouette 0.491) and the highest classification performance (NN Accuracy 94.6%, F1 0.962), while excluding these actions reduced both clustering coherence and predictive efficiency. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.performance_metrics: Neural Network (Doc2Vec vectors): F1 0.862–0.917 and Accuracy 0.862–0.876 across cases; best case (Case 2: mail-sending + environment actions) F1 0.917 and Accuracy 0.876. Random Forest: F1 0.761–0.923 and Accuracy 0.652–0.880 across cases; best case (Case 2) F1 0.923 and Accuracy 0.880. Unsupervised verification: silhouette score 0.180 (Case 1 all actions), 0.333 (Case 2 mail-sending+environment), 0.129 (Case 3 excluding mail-sending+environment).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PIAAC 2012 “Club Membership” log the study leveraged action-sequence representations (Word2Vec/Doc2Vec) to predict correct vs incorrect performance, finding that the subset of mail-sending and environment actions (Case 2) yielded the best cluster separation (silhouette 0.333) and strong classification performance (NN F1 0.917, Accuracy 0.876), whereas removing these actions decreased separation and predictive efficiency. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzes 2012 PIAAC log data for two problem sets (“Party Invitation” and “Club Membership”) and uses Word2Vec/Doc2Vec to vectorize action sequences, then applies neural networks and random forests to classify performance groups. For “Party Invitation,” the best configuration (Case 2 using mail-moving actions only) achieved NN Accuracy of 94.6% with F1 of 0.962, alongside the highest silhouette score (0.491), while excluding mail-moving actions reduced both clustering coherence and predictive performance (silhouette 0.216; NN Accuracy 0.850). For “Club Membership,” Case 2 (mail-sending + environment actions) produced NN F1 of 0.917 and Accuracy of 0.876 with silhouette 0.333, whereas Case 3 (excluding key actions) lowered separation (silhouette 0.129). The paper removes incomplete/missing-action sequences (listwise deletion) and does not report PV handling or survey-weight application; therefore, results should be interpreted as predictive classification of log-derived performance levels rather than PV-based achievement, and generalizability may be limited by the single-country Belgian PIAAC dataset and the lack of explicit complex-survey weighting.
